title: 2  1. Data set up
text:
7.1 Data management

7.1.1 Clean and select subset of data
Factorise categorical data and removes labelled values from ones I will treat as numeric.

df2 <- df %>% mutate(
  RAgeCat = to_factor(RAgeCat), 
  RSex = to_factor(RSex), 
  skipmeal = to_factor(skipmeal),
  MuchPov = to_factor(MuchPov),
  leftrigh = remove_labels(leftrigh, keep_var_label = T, user_na_to_na = T), 
  welfare2 = remove_labels(welfare2, keep_var_label = T, user_na_to_na = T),
  libauth = remove_labels(libauth, keep_var_label = T, user_na_to_na = T),
  WtFactor = remove_labels(WtFactor, keep_var_label = T, user_na_to_na = T)) %>% 
  select(RAgeCat, RSex, skipmeal, leftrigh, welfare2, libauth, MuchPov, WtFactor)

title: 2  Set up
text:
2.2 Folder
The code below will set file paths to load data.

source("Set file paths.R")

At present it really only does this, but more may be added later.

ddir <- "/Users/joecrowley/R/Data/BSA Teaching Data/spss/spss25"
load(paste0(ddir, "/Multicode data.Rds"))
load(paste0(ddir, "/Clean BSA data.Rds"))

title: 2  Set up
2  Set up
text:
2.4 Function to save functions

write_function <- function(func_name, folder) {
  
  # Get the name of the function as a string
  func_name_str <- deparse(substitute(func_name))
  
  # Build the assignment line
  assign_statement <- paste0(func_name_str, " <- ")
  
  # Build the file path
  file_name <- file.path(folder, paste0(func_name_str, ".R"))
  
  # Get the function object itself
  function_to_export <- get(func_name_str)
  
  # Write to file
  writeLines(
    c(assign_statement, deparse(function_to_export)),
    con = file_name
  )
}

write_function(df_to_dense_flextable, folder = "Scripts")
write_function(write_function, folder = "Scripts") # Save out itself for later use

5RAgeCatAge of respondent(grouped)<7 category> dvdbl+lbl6NULLc(`Item not applicable` = -1, `18-24` = 1, `25-34` = 2, `35-44` = 3, `45-54` = 4, `55-59` = 5, `60-64` = 6, `65+` = 7, `DK/Refused/Not answered` = 8)

6MuchPovSome people say there is very little real poverty in Britain today - Others say there is quite a lot -  Which comes closest to your viewdbl+lbl135NULLc(`Item not applicable` = -1, `... that there is very little real poverty in Britain,` = 1, `or, that there is quite a lot?` = 2, `Don`t know` = 8, Refusal = 9)

title: 2  Set up
text:
2.7 Create multicode test data

set.seed(123)
n <- 100  # total respondents

# ----- Five multi-coded Yes/No factor variables -----
survey_data <- data.frame(
  Q1_OptionA = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionB = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionC = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionD = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionE = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
)

# Add new multi-coded variables Q2_ChoiceA–E
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceA <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceB <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceC <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceD <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceE <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))


# ----- Add two non-multi-coded variables -----
# Age group (everyone answered)
survey_data$AgeGroup <- factor(
  sample(c("18–24", "25–34", "35–44", "45+"), n, replace = TRUE),
  levels = c("18–24", "25–34", "35–44", "45+")
)

# Satisfaction (missing values for some respondents → smaller base size)
survey_data$Satisfaction <- factor(
  sample(c("Very satisfied", "Satisfied", "Neutral", "Dissatisfied", "Very dissatisfied", NA), 
         n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.15, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10)),
  levels = c("Very satisfied", "Satisfied", "Neutral", "Dissatisfied", "Very dissatisfied")
)


# Satisfaction (missing values for some respondents → smaller base size)
survey_data$Sex <- factor(
  sample(c("Male", "Female", NA), 
         n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.49, 0.49, 0.02)),
  levels = c("Male", "Female")
)

# ----- Add variable labels -----
var_label(survey_data) <- list(
  Q1_OptionA = "Q1: Selected Option A",
  Q1_OptionB = "Q1: Selected Option B",
  Q1_OptionC = "Q1: Selected Option C",
  Q1_OptionD = "Q1: Selected Option D",
  Q1_OptionE = "Q1: Selected Option E",
  Q2_ChoiceA = "Q2: Chose Option A",
  Q2_ChoiceB = "Q2: Chose Option B",
  Q2_ChoiceC = "Q2: Chose Option C",
  Q2_ChoiceD = "Q2: Chose Option D",
  Q2_ChoiceE = "Q2: Chose Option E",
  AgeGroup = "Respondent age group",
  Satisfaction = "Overall satisfaction with service", 
  Sex = "Gender"
)

# ----- Check -----
str(survey_data)

text:
3.2 Function to standardise variable names
Purpose: takes the predictor variable names and labels from column names to appearing in their own columns with standardised names, e.g. p_cat1 for the first predictor variable’s categories.

3.2.1 Test data 1

example_table <- 
  df_svy %>% group_by(across(all_of(c("RAgeCat", "RSex")))) %>% 
  summarise(leftrigh = mean(leftrigh, na.rm = T))

3.2.3 Function

standardise_names <- 
  function(
    data, # dataframe used to create the table in question
    out_var, # outcome variable
    preds, # Grouping predictor variables
    table # Table containing variables to be standardised 
    )    {
    
    if(any(str_detect(class(data), "survey"))){data_to_look_in <- data[["variables"]]
    } else {data_to_look_in <- data}
        
    # Create a standardised set of variable names
    standardised_variable_names <- c(paste0("p_cat", seq_along(preds)), "o_cat")

    renamed_variables <- table %>%
          # Rename df columns with standard names
          rename(any_of(setNames(c(preds,out_var), standardised_variable_names)))  %>% 
          # Add outcome variable label
          mutate(o_lab = attributes(data_to_look_in[[out_var]])$label) %>%
          # Add column with outcome variable name
          mutate(outcome = out_var) 

        # Adding predictor meta data
        all_renamed <- map(seq_len(length(preds)), function(number) {

          # for each predictor in turn - create standardised variable names
          standardised_predictor_names <- paste0(c("predictor", "p_lab"), number)

          # Extract predictor variable labels
          p_label <- attributes(data_to_look_in[[preds[number]]])$label

          # Handle missing variable labels - not been required
          if(is.null(p_label)){p_label <- preds[number]}

          # Add predictor variable names and labels to ctab
          ctab_plus_new_labels <- renamed_variables %>%
            mutate(predictor = preds[number],
                   p_lab = p_label) %>%
            # Rename predictor variables with standardised names
            rename(any_of(setNames(c("predictor", "p_lab"), standardised_predictor_names)))

          return(ctab_plus_new_labels)

           }) %>% reduce(., full_join)
    
    return(all_renamed)
        
  }

write_function(standardise_names, folder = "Scripts")

standardise_names(data = df_svy, 
                  table = example_table, 
                  preds = c("RAgeCat", "RSex"),

title: 3  Helper functions
text:
3.3 Base descriptions
To work with base descriptions three functions are required:

base_information - which creates a library of base descriptions for use when creating tables.
create_bases - which uses the output of base_information to know what base descriptions to put with each table.
prepare_base_for_table - takes the output of create_bases, stored within the resulting data frame, and creates a more human-readable output when formatting the resulting tables for presentation.



3.3.1 Function to create base descriptions
Purpose - creates an object you need in your environment to create tables with base sizes - it contains the variable base descriptions to use. The output is taken as an argument by functions for calculating statistics used later in the process.
Arguments.

data - the data frame containing the variables.

general_base - a character vector. A general base description for use with most variables.

specific_bases - a named character vector. Names are variable names. Content is a base description.

Where a variable has now corresponding description in specific_bases then general_base is used.

base_information <- function(
    data, 
    general_base, 
    specific_bases
) {
  
  everything_else <- general_base
  var_descriptions <- specific_bases
  
  variable_labels <- data %>% select(names(var_descriptions)) %>% var_label(unlist = T, null_action = "fill")
  all_variable_labels <- data %>% var_label(unlist = T, null_action = "fill")
  
  base_descriptions <- list(everything_else, var_descriptions, variable_labels, all_variable_labels)
  return(base_descriptions)
  
}

write_function(base_information, folder = "Scripts")


The argument to specific_bases should look like this.

var_descriptions <- rep("Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents", 6)
names(var_descriptions) <- c("leftrigh", "welfare2", "libauth", "libauth2", "leftrigh2", "welfare3") 
var_descriptions

                                                                 leftrigh 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                 welfare2 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                  libauth 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                 libauth2 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                leftrigh2 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                 welfare3 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 



Here the function is applied to the BSA data.
The result is a list:

Character vector, general base description for all variables without a specific description.

Named character vector, with base descriptions for specific variables.

Variable labels for variables with a specific description.

Variable labels for all variables in the data frame.


example_of_base_information <- base_information(
  data = df2,
  general_base = "Asked of all respondents", 
  specific_bases = var_descriptions
)

3.3.2 Create base info within tables
Purpose: This is the function used within larger functions calculating statistics (mean)
create_bases takes two arguments:

base_info is the output of the base_information() function.

variables is a character vector of variable names.

It exports a character vector which contains the base description for each variable name. If base descriptions are repeated, at this point they are simply duplicated in the output vector. Where there are multiple variables, their descriptions are separated by ” X “.

create_bases <- function(
    base_info,
    variables
){
  
  # Extract the names of variables that have specific bases defined (from base_info[[2]])
  names_of_variables_with_specific_bases <- base_info[[2]] %>% names
  
  # Logical vector: TRUE if variable has a specific base and is in 'variables', FALSE otherwise
  bases_to_use <- names_of_variables_with_specific_bases %in% variables
    
  # Get the labels of the variables that match bases_to_use (from base_info[[3]])
  variable_labels_to_use <- base_info[[3]][bases_to_use]
  
  # Get the specific bases that match bases_to_use (from base_info[[2]])
  bases_to_use <- base_info[[2]][bases_to_use] 
  
  # If there are any variables with specific bases...
  if(length(bases_to_use) > 0){
    # Create base strings in the form "<base>:-  <label>"
    list_of_bases <- paste0(bases_to_use, ":-  ", variable_labels_to_use)
  } else {
    # If no specific bases apply, start with an empty vector
    list_of_bases <- c()
  }
    
  # Find variables in 'variables' that do NOT have specific bases
  variables_using_default_description <- variables[!variables %in% names_of_variables_with_specific_bases]
  
  # If there are any variables without specific bases...
  if(length(variables_using_default_description) > 0){
    # Get their labels (from base_info[[4]])
    labels_of_variables_using_default_description <- base_info[[4]][variables_using_default_description]
    
    # Create default base strings in the form "<general_base>:-  <label>"
    default_base_description <- paste0(base_info[[1]],  ":-  ", labels_of_variables_using_default_description)
    
    # Append the default descriptions to the list_of_bases
    list_of_bases <- c(list_of_bases, default_base_description)
    } 
  
  # Collapse all base strings into one single string separated by " X "
  list_of_bases <- list_of_bases %>% paste0(., collapse = " X ")
  
  return(list_of_bases) # Return the final string

}

write_function(create_bases, folder = "Scripts")


These examples of applying create_bases show different resulting combinations of base descriptions.

create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "RSex"))

[1] "Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents:-  Left-right scale (redistrb to indust4) dv X Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents:-  Libertarian-authoritarian scale (TradVals to censor) dv X Asked of all respondents:-  Sex of respondent"

create_bases(example_of_base_information, "RSex")

[1] "Asked of all respondents:-  Sex of respondent"

create_bases(example_of_base_information, "skipmeal2")

[1] "Asked of all respondents:-  How often do you or other members of your household skip a meal because there is not enough money for food?: SC B, C"

create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","skipmeal2","RAgeCat","RSex"))

[1] "Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents:-  Libertarian-authoritarian scale (TradVals to censor) dv X Asked of all respondents:-  How often do you or other members of your household skip a meal because there is not enough money for food?: SC B, C X Asked of all respondents:-  Age of respondent(grouped)<7 category> dv X Asked of all respondents:-  Sex of respondent"




3.3.2.1 Prepare base for table
The left hand side of each base description states the population it applies to, the right hand side which variable it relates to.
First split these in two, and see if the left hand side resolves into a single unique value.

base_test1 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "RSex"))
base_test2 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh"))
base_test3 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh","welfare2"))
base_test4 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "welfare2", "RSex", "RAgeCat"))





3.3.3 Create formatted bases for display under a table
Purpose: This takes the long string of base descriptions returned by create_bases and makes into a human-readable piece of text.
Why not do this all in one go, as part of create_bases? Because, this returns a description for all variables in a table, and sometimes, the output of calc_stats, the function create_bases is used within, is later broken up into sub-tables. This stage happens after that, once the final variables to be shown in a table is known.
Arguments: it has only one argument, bases_for_table, which should be the character vector returned by create_bases.
Outputs: the outputs come as a list, each element corresponding to a different base description.

prepare_base_for_table <- function(bases_for_table) {
  
  base_results <- list()  # Empty list to store results

  # Split the input string by " X " into separate base elements, then keep only unique elements
  bases <- bases_for_table %>% str_split_1(., " X ") %>% unique
  
  # From each base element, extract the part before ":-  " as base description
  base_descriptions <- bases %>% str_split_i(., ":-  ", 1)
  
  # From each base element, extract the part after ":-  " as variable label(s)
  variables <- bases %>% str_split_i(., ":-  ", 2)
  
  # Find all unique base descriptions
  unique_base_descriptions <- unique(base_descriptions)
  
  # If there is only one unique base description...
  if(length(unique_base_descriptions) == 1){
    # Append that unique base description to the results list
    base_results <- append(base_results ,list(unique_base_descriptions))
    } else {
       
  # Create a data frame of `base_descriptions` and count the occurrences of each
  most_common_base_description <- data.frame(base_descriptions = base_descriptions) %>% 
    count(base_descriptions) %>%   # Count frequency of each base description
    mutate(max_n = max(n)) %>%     # Add a column with max count value
    filter(n == max_n) %>%         # Filter to base description(s) with max count
    pull(base_descriptions) %>%    # Extract the base description(s)
    unique                         # Remove duplicates if any
  
  # If there are multiple most common base descriptions, keep only the first one
  if(length(most_common_base_description) > 1){
    most_common_base_description <- most_common_base_description[1]
    }
  
  # Format the most common base description prepended by "All other variables:- " 
  base_results[[1]] <- most_common_base_description %>% 
    paste("All other variables:- ", ., collapse = "") %>% 
    str_to_sentence() # and capitalize sentence
  
  # Remove the most common base description from the unique list to handle separately
  unique_base_descriptions <- unique_base_descriptions[!unique_base_descriptions %in% most_common_base_description]
  
  # Loop over remaining unique base descriptions
  for (xxx in unique_base_descriptions){
    
    # Find indices where the base description matches the current unique description
    index <- which(base_descriptions %in% xxx) 
    
    # Concatenate the variables associated with this base description into a comma-separated string
    # `index` identifies the relevant variable labels from `variables`
    variable_list <- paste0(variables[index], collapse = ", ") 
    
    # Create a string combining the base description and its variables
    variable_description <- paste0(xxx, ":- ", variable_list) 
    
    # Append this description to the results list
    base_results <- append(base_results, variable_description)
    
  }
  
  }

    # Most common last. 
    base_results <- rev(base_results)
    
  # Return the list of prepared base descriptions
    return(base_results)

}

write_function(prepare_base_for_table, folder = "Scripts")


Example applications of prepare_base_for_table.

title: 3  Helper functions
text:
3.4 Support functions for unweighted_median


3.4.1 A function for grouped median SE
Purpose: this function willb used with unweighted_median to calculate an unweighted standard error of a median within groups.
Variables to use for testing…

df2 %>% names

 [1] "RAgeCat"   "RSex"      "skipmeal"  "leftrigh"  "welfare2"  "libauth"  
 [7] "MuchPov"   "WtFactor"  "skipmeal2" "MuchPov2"  "libauth2"  "leftrigh2"
[13] "welfare3" 

df2 %>% count(RSex, RAgeCat) %>% df_to_dense_flextable()

RSexRAgeCatnMale18-2491Male25-34174Male35-44227Male45-54241Male55-59125Male60-64134Male65+460MaleDK/Refused/Not answered2Female18-24111Female25-34255Female35-44298Female45-54302Female55-59124Female60-64127Female65+549FemaleDK/Refused/Not answered4



Arguments:

data - data frame in use by unweighted_median,

outcome - character vector length 1,

predictors - character vector of predictors.


grouped_medianse <- function(data, outcome, predictors){
  
  # Select only the predictor columns from the data
  data_with_required_predictors <- data %>% select(all_of(predictors)) 
  
  # For each predictor column, pull the factor vector from the data and drop unused levels
  predictors_as_list <- map(
    names(data_with_required_predictors), 
    ~data %>% pull(.x) %>% fct_drop
  )
  
  # Split the original data into subsets based on combinations of predictor factor levels
  split_data <- split(data, predictors_as_list, drop = TRUE)
  
  # For each subset of data, calculate the median standard error and create a results row
  results <- map(1:length(split_data), function(number) {
    
    # Extract the outcome variable values for the current subset
    outcome_data <- split_data[[number]] %>% pull(outcome)
    
    # Calculate the median standard error using the wrappedtools package
    median <- wrappedtools::medianse(outcome_data)
    
    # Extract the unique combination of predictor levels for this subset
    levels_in_use <- split_data[[number]] %>% 
      select(all_of(predictors)) %>% 
      unique() %>% 
      # Add a new column with the median standard error estimate
      mutate(estimate_se = median)
    
  }) %>% 
    
    bind_rows() # Combine all results into a single data frame
  
  return(results) # Return the combined data frame with predictor levels and median SE estimates
  
}

write_function(grouped_medianse, folder = "Scripts")


Examples of application, the output is a dataframe with the estimated standard error with the desired naming convention estimate_se which matches that used elsewhere for calculating SE.

3.4.2 A function for grouped median CI
Purpose: this function willb used with unweighted_median to calculate an unweighted confidence interval around a median within groups.
Arguments:

data - data frame in use by unweighted_median,

outcome - character vector length 1,

predictors - character vector of predictors.


grouped_medianci <- function(data, outcome, predictors){
  
  # Select only the predictor columns from the data
  data_with_required_predictors <- data %>% select(all_of(predictors)) 
  
  # For each predictor column, extract its factor values from data and drop unused levels
  predictors_as_list <- map(
    names(data_with_required_predictors), 
    ~data %>% pull(.x) %>% fct_drop
  )
  
  # Split the original data frame into subsets based on the combination of factor levels in predictors_as_list
  split_data <- split(data, predictors_as_list, drop = TRUE)
  
  # For each subset in split_data...
  results <- map(1:length(split_data), function(number) {
    
    # Pull the outcome variable values for this subset
    outcome_data <- split_data[[number]] %>% pull(outcome)
    
    # Proceed only if there are more than 5 observations in the subset
    if(length(outcome_data) > 5){
      
      # Calculate median confidence interval with bootstrap (from wrappedtools package)
      cil <- wrappedtools::median_cl_boot(outcome_data)
      
      # Extract the unique combination of predictor levels for this subset
      levels_in_use <- split_data[[number]] %>% 
        select(all_of(predictors)) %>% 
        unique() %>% 
        # Add the lower and upper confidence interval bounds as new columns
        mutate(estimate_low = cil$CIlow, estimate_upp = cil$CIhigh)
      
      # Return this data frame with predictor levels and confidence intervals
      return(levels_in_use) 
    }
  }) %>% 
    # Combine the list of data frames into one data frame
    bind_rows()
  
  # Return the combined results
  return(results)
  
}

write_function(grouped_medianci, folder = "Scripts")

Again, the output is a dataframe with the estimated CI using the naming convention unweighted_median expects, which matches that used elsewhere for calculating CI.

title: 3  Helper functions
text:
3.5 Significance testing
Data filtered on both outcome and predictor variables.
Predictor already a factor. Outcome either already numeric or a factor.
unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat:
* “mean”
* “median”
* “sum”
unweighted_test_cat_by_cat:
* “perc”
* “count”
weighted_test_numeric_by_cat:
* “w_mean”
* “w_median”
* “w_sum”
weighted_test_cat_by_cat:
* “w_perc”
* “w_count”

3.5.1 Numeric variables, unweighted

# df2
# data <- df3
# outcome <- "skipmeal2"
# predictor <- "RSex"

unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x <- data %>% pull(predictor)
  y <- data %>% pull(outcome)
  
  x_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  complete_idx <- complete.cases(y, x)
  y <- y[complete_idx]
  x <- x[complete_idx]
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    # Check normality of residuals (Shapiro-Wilk)
    model <- lm(y ~ x)
    shapiro_p <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value
    
    # Decision
    if (shapiro_p < 0.05) {
      # Non-normal → Kruskal–Wallis
      method_used <- "Kruskal–Wallis"
      p_val <- kruskal.test(y ~ x)$p.value
    } else {
      # Normal enough → Welch's ANOVA
      method_used <- "Welch's ANOVA"
      p_val <- oneway.test(y ~ x, var.equal = FALSE)$p.value
    }
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
    cross_break = predictor,
    predictor1 = predictor, 
    p_lab1 = x_lab, 
    outcome = outcome, 
    o_lab = y_lab,
    p_method = result$method,
    p_value = result$p
  )
  
  result <- map_df(c("mean", "median", "sum"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
}

3.5.2 Numeric variables, weighted

# data <- df_svy
# outcome <- "welfare2"
# predictor <- "RSex"

weighted_test_numeric_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  suppressMessages({
  
  x_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  filtered_data <- data %>% filter(if_all(c(outcome, predictor), ~!is.na(.x)))
  frmla <- paste0(outcome, " ~ ", predictor)
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    # Check normality of residuals (Shapiro-Wilk)
    model <- survey::svyglm(frmla, design = filtered_data)
    shapiro_p <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value
    
    # Decision
    if (shapiro_p < 0.05) {
      # Non-normal → Kruskal–Wallis
      method_used <- "Kruskal–Wallis"
      p_val <- survey::svyranktest(frmla, design = filtered_data)$p.value
    } else {
      # Normal enough → Welch's ANOVA
      method_used <- "Wald Test"
      anova_result <- survey::regTermTest(model, predictor)
      p_val <- anova_result[["p"]] %>% as.vector()
    }
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
      cross_break = predictor,
      predictor1 = predictor, 
      p_lab1 = x_lab, 
      outcome = outcome, 
      o_lab = y_lab,
      p_method = result$method,
      p_value = result$p
    )
  
  result <- map_df(c("w_mean", "w_median", "w_sum"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
  })
}

write_function(weighted_test_numeric_by_cat, folder = "Scripts")


weighted_test_numeric_by_cat(df_svy, outcome = "welfare2", predictor = "RSex")

3.5.3 Categorical variables, unweighted

unweighted_test_cat_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x <- data %>% pull(predictor)
  y <- data %>% pull(outcome)
  
  x_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  complete_idx <- complete.cases(y, x)
  y <- y[complete_idx]
  x <- x[complete_idx]
  
  result <- tryCatch({

      # chi-square test
      method_used <- "Chi-Square test"
      p_val <- chisq.test(x, y)$p.value
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
      cross_break = predictor,
      predictor1 = predictor, 
      p_lab1 = x_lab, 
      outcome = outcome, 
      o_lab = y_lab,
      p_method = result$method,
      p_value = result$p
     )

  result <- map_df(c("perc", "count"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)

}

write_function(unweighted_test_cat_by_cat, folder = "Scripts")

3.5.4 Categorical variables, weighted

# data <- df_svy
# outcome <- "MuchPov"
# predictor <- "RSex"

# test <- svychisq(~MuchPov + RAgeCat, df_svy, statistic = "adjWald")

weighted_test_cat_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  filtered_data <- data %>% filter(if_all(c(outcome, predictor), ~!is.na(.x)))
  frmla <- formula(paste0("~",outcome,  " + " , predictor))
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    
      # chi-square test
      method_used <- "Chi-Square test"
      p_val <- svychisq(frmla, design = filtered_data, statistic = "adjWald")$p.value
      p_val <- p_val %>% as.vector()
      
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
    cross_break = predictor,
    predictor1 = predictor, 
    p_lab1 = x_lab, 
    outcome = outcome, 
    o_lab = y_lab,
    p_method = result$method,
    p_value = result$p
  )

  result <- map_df(c("w_perc", "w_count"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
}

write_function(weighted_test_cat_by_cat, folder = "Scripts")

# data <- df_svy
# outcome <- "libauth"
# predictor <- "RAgeCat"
# 
# rm(data)
# rm(outcome)
# rm(predictor)

return_pvalues <- function(data, outcome, predictor){

    if(any(str_detect(class(data), "survey"))){
      data_2 <- data[["variables"]]
      weights <- "weights"
    } else {
        data_2 <- data
        weights <- "no weights"
    }
  
  outcome_class_factor <- class(data_2 %>% pull(outcome)) %>% str_detect(., "factor") %>% any()
  
  if(weights == "no weights"){
    
    if(outcome_class_factor == TRUE){
      result <- unweighted_test_cat_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    } else { 
      result <- unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    }
    
  } else {
    
    if(outcome_class_factor == TRUE){
      result <- weighted_test_cat_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor) 
    } else { 
      result <- weighted_test_numeric_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    }
    
  }
  
  return(result)

}

write_function(return_pvalues, folder = "Scripts")

3.6.1 Map p.values wrapper

outcomes <- c("libauth", "RSex", "RAgeCat")
predictors <- c("MuchPov2", "welfare3")

map_return_p_values <- function(outcomes, predictors, data){
  map_df(predictors, function(pred){
    map_df(outcomes, function(out){
      return_pvalues(data = data, outcome = out, predictor = pred)
    })
  })
}


write_function(map_return_p_values, folder = "Scripts")

title: 2  Set up
2  Set up
text:
2.4 Function to save functions

write_function <- function(func_name, folder) {
  
  # Get the name of the function as a string
  func_name_str <- deparse(substitute(func_name))
  
  # Build the assignment line
  assign_statement <- paste0(func_name_str, " <- ")
  
  # Build the file path
  file_name <- file.path(folder, paste0(func_name_str, ".R"))
  
  # Get the function object itself
  function_to_export <- get(func_name_str)
  
  # Write to file
  writeLines(
    c(assign_statement, deparse(function_to_export)),
    con = file_name
  )
}

write_function(df_to_dense_flextable, folder = "Scripts")
write_function(write_function, folder = "Scripts") # Save out itself for later use

title: 2  Set up

2.5 Function to load functions

source_folder_r <- function(folder, verbose = FALSE) {

  r_files <- list.files(folder, pattern = "\\.R$", full.names = TRUE)

  if (verbose) {
    cat("Sourcing files:\n")
    print(basename(r_files))
  }

  invisible(lapply(r_files, source))
}

title: 2  Set up
text:
2.7 Create multicode test data

set.seed(123)
n <- 100  # total respondents

# ----- Five multi-coded Yes/No factor variables -----
survey_data <- data.frame(
  Q1_OptionA = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionB = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionC = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionD = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  Q1_OptionE = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
)

# Add new multi-coded variables Q2_ChoiceA–E
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceA <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceB <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceC <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceD <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))
survey_data$Q2_ChoiceE <- factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No"))


# ----- Add two non-multi-coded variables -----
# Age group (everyone answered)
survey_data$AgeGroup <- factor(
  sample(c("18–24", "25–34", "35–44", "45+"), n, replace = TRUE),
  levels = c("18–24", "25–34", "35–44", "45+")
)

# Satisfaction (missing values for some respondents → smaller base size)
survey_data$Satisfaction <- factor(
  sample(c("Very satisfied", "Satisfied", "Neutral", "Dissatisfied", "Very dissatisfied", NA), 
         n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.15, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10)),
  levels = c("Very satisfied", "Satisfied", "Neutral", "Dissatisfied", "Very dissatisfied")
)


# Satisfaction (missing values for some respondents → smaller base size)
survey_data$Sex <- factor(
  sample(c("Male", "Female", NA), 
         n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.49, 0.49, 0.02)),
  levels = c("Male", "Female")
)

# ----- Add variable labels -----
var_label(survey_data) <- list(
  Q1_OptionA = "Q1: Selected Option A",
  Q1_OptionB = "Q1: Selected Option B",
  Q1_OptionC = "Q1: Selected Option C",
  Q1_OptionD = "Q1: Selected Option D",
  Q1_OptionE = "Q1: Selected Option E",
  Q2_ChoiceA = "Q2: Chose Option A",
  Q2_ChoiceB = "Q2: Chose Option B",
  Q2_ChoiceC = "Q2: Chose Option C",
  Q2_ChoiceD = "Q2: Chose Option D",
  Q2_ChoiceE = "Q2: Chose Option E",
  AgeGroup = "Respondent age group",
  Satisfaction = "Overall satisfaction with service", 
  Sex = "Gender"
)

# ----- Check -----
str(survey_data)

title: 4  Helper functions
text:
4.2 Function to standardise variable names
Purpose: takes the predictor variable names and labels from column names to appearing in their own columns with standardised names, e.g. p_cat1 for the first predictor variable’s categories.

4.2.1 Test data 1

example_table <- 
  df_svy %>% group_by(across(all_of(c("RAgeCat", "RSex")))) %>% 
  summarise(leftrigh = mean(leftrigh, na.rm = T))

`.groups` argument.

4.2.3 Function

standardise_names <- 
  function(
    data, # dataframe used to create the table in question
    out_var, # outcome variable
    preds, # Grouping predictor variables
    table # Table containing variables to be standardised 
    )    {
    
    if(any(str_detect(class(data), "survey"))){data_to_look_in <- data[["variables"]]
    } else {data_to_look_in <- data}
        
    # Create a standardised set of variable names
    standardised_variable_names <- c(paste0("p_cat", seq_along(preds)), "o_cat")

    renamed_variables <- table %>%
          # Rename df columns with standard names
          rename(any_of(setNames(c(preds,out_var), standardised_variable_names)))  %>% 
          # Add outcome variable label
          mutate(o_lab = attributes(data_to_look_in[[out_var]])$label) %>%
          # Add column with outcome variable name
          mutate(outcome = out_var) 

        # Adding predictor meta data
        all_renamed <- map(seq_len(length(preds)), function(number) {

          # for each predictor in turn - create standardised variable names
          standardised_predictor_names <- paste0(c("predictor", "p_lab"), number)

          # Extract predictor variable labels
          p_label <- attributes(data_to_look_in[[preds[number]]])$label

          # Handle missing variable labels - not been required
          if(is.null(p_label)){p_label <- preds[number]}

          # Add predictor variable names and labels to ctab
          ctab_plus_new_labels <- renamed_variables %>%
            mutate(predictor = preds[number],
                   p_lab = p_label) %>%
            # Rename predictor variables with standardised names
            rename(any_of(setNames(c("predictor", "p_lab"), standardised_predictor_names)))

          return(ctab_plus_new_labels)

           }) %>% reduce(., full_join)
    
    return(all_renamed)
        
  }

write_function(standardise_names, folder = "Scripts")

standardise_names(data = df_svy, 
                  table = example_table, 
                  preds = c("RAgeCat", "RSex"),

title: 4  Helper functions
text:
4.3 Base descriptions
To work with base descriptions three functions are required:

base_information - which creates a library of base descriptions for use when creating tables.
create_bases - which uses the output of base_information to know what base descriptions to put with each table.
prepare_base_for_table - takes the output of create_bases, stored within the resulting data frame, and creates a more human-readable output when formatting the resulting tables for presentation.



4.3.1 Function to create base descriptions
Purpose - creates an object you need in your environment to create tables with base sizes - it contains the variable base descriptions to use. The output is taken as an argument by functions for calculating statistics used later in the process.
Arguments.

data - the data frame containing the variables.

general_base - a character vector. A general base description for use with most variables.

specific_bases - a named character vector. Names are variable names. Content is a base description.

Where a variable has now corresponding description in specific_bases then general_base is used.

base_information <- function(
    data, 
    general_base, 
    specific_bases
) {
  
  everything_else <- general_base
  var_descriptions <- specific_bases
  
  variable_labels <- data %>% select(names(var_descriptions)) %>% var_label(unlist = T, null_action = "fill")
  all_variable_labels <- data %>% var_label(unlist = T, null_action = "fill")
  
  base_descriptions <- list(everything_else, var_descriptions, variable_labels, all_variable_labels)
  return(base_descriptions)
  
}

write_function(base_information, folder = "Scripts")


The argument to specific_bases should look like this.

var_descriptions <- rep("Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents", 6)
names(var_descriptions) <- c("leftrigh", "welfare2", "libauth", "libauth2", "leftrigh2", "welfare3") 
var_descriptions

"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents" 
                                                                 libauth2 
"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents"

"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents"

"Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents"

example_of_base_information <- base_information(
  data = df2,

)

4.3.2 Create base info within tables
Purpose: This is the function used within larger functions calculating statistics (mean)
create_bases takes two arguments:

base_info is the output of the base_information() function.

variables is a character vector of variable names.

It exports a character vector which contains the base description for each variable name. If base descriptions are repeated, at this point they are simply duplicated in the output vector. Where there are multiple variables, their descriptions are separated by ” X “.

create_bases <- function(
    base_info,
    variables
){
  
  # Extract the names of variables that have specific bases defined (from base_info[[2]])
  names_of_variables_with_specific_bases <- base_info[[2]] %>% names
  
  # Logical vector: TRUE if variable has a specific base and is in 'variables', FALSE otherwise
  bases_to_use <- names_of_variables_with_specific_bases %in% variables
    
  # Get the labels of the variables that match bases_to_use (from base_info[[3]])
  variable_labels_to_use <- base_info[[3]][bases_to_use]
  
  # Get the specific bases that match bases_to_use (from base_info[[2]])
  bases_to_use <- base_info[[2]][bases_to_use] 
  
  # If there are any variables with specific bases...
  if(length(bases_to_use) > 0){
    # Create base strings in the form "<base>:-  <label>"
    list_of_bases <- paste0(bases_to_use, ":-  ", variable_labels_to_use)
  } else {
    # If no specific bases apply, start with an empty vector
    list_of_bases <- c()
  }
    
  # Find variables in 'variables' that do NOT have specific bases
  variables_using_default_description <- variables[!variables %in% names_of_variables_with_specific_bases]
  
  # If there are any variables without specific bases...
  if(length(variables_using_default_description) > 0){
    # Get their labels (from base_info[[4]])
    labels_of_variables_using_default_description <- base_info[[4]][variables_using_default_description]
    
    # Create default base strings in the form "<general_base>:-  <label>"
    default_base_description <- paste0(base_info[[1]],  ":-  ", labels_of_variables_using_default_description)
    
    # Append the default descriptions to the list_of_bases
    list_of_bases <- c(list_of_bases, default_base_description)
    } 
  
  # Collapse all base strings into one single string separated by " X "
  list_of_bases <- list_of_bases %>% paste0(., collapse = " X ")
  
  return(list_of_bases) # Return the final string

}

write_function(create_bases, folder = "Scripts")


These examples of applying create_bases show different resulting combinations of base descriptions.

create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "RSex"))

4.3.2.1 Prepare base for table
The left hand side of each base description states the population it applies to, the right hand side which variable it relates to.
First split these in two, and see if the left hand side resolves into a single unique value.

base_test1 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "RSex"))
base_test2 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh"))
base_test3 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh","welfare2"))
base_test4 <- create_bases(example_of_base_information, c("libauth","leftrigh", "welfare2", "RSex", "RAgeCat"))





4.3.3 Create formatted bases for display under a table
Purpose: This takes the long string of base descriptions returned by create_bases and makes into a human-readable piece of text.
Why not do this all in one go, as part of create_bases? Because, this returns a description for all variables in a table, and sometimes, the output of calc_stats, the function create_bases is used within, is later broken up into sub-tables. This stage happens after that, once the final variables to be shown in a table is known.
Arguments: it has only one argument, bases_for_table, which should be the character vector returned by create_bases.
Outputs: the outputs come as a list, each element corresponding to a different base description.

prepare_base_for_table <- function(bases_for_table) {
  
  base_results <- list()  # Empty list to store results

  # Split the input string by " X " into separate base elements, then keep only unique elements
  bases <- bases_for_table %>% str_split_1(., " X ") %>% unique
  
  # From each base element, extract the part before ":-  " as base description
  base_descriptions <- bases %>% str_split_i(., ":-  ", 1)
  
  # From each base element, extract the part after ":-  " as variable label(s)
  variables <- bases %>% str_split_i(., ":-  ", 2)
  
  # Find all unique base descriptions
  unique_base_descriptions <- unique(base_descriptions)
  
  # If there is only one unique base description...
  if(length(unique_base_descriptions) == 1){
    # Append that unique base description to the results list
    base_results <- append(base_results ,list(unique_base_descriptions))
    } else {
       
  # Create a data frame of `base_descriptions` and count the occurrences of each
  most_common_base_description <- data.frame(base_descriptions = base_descriptions) %>% 
    count(base_descriptions) %>%   # Count frequency of each base description
    mutate(max_n = max(n)) %>%     # Add a column with max count value
    filter(n == max_n) %>%         # Filter to base description(s) with max count
    pull(base_descriptions) %>%    # Extract the base description(s)
    unique                         # Remove duplicates if any
  
  # If there are multiple most common base descriptions, keep only the first one
  if(length(most_common_base_description) > 1){
    most_common_base_description <- most_common_base_description[1]
    }
  
  # Format the most common base description prepended by "All other variables:- " 
  base_results[[1]] <- most_common_base_description %>% 
    paste("All other variables:- ", ., collapse = "") %>% 
    str_to_sentence() # and capitalize sentence
  
  # Remove the most common base description from the unique list to handle separately
  unique_base_descriptions <- unique_base_descriptions[!unique_base_descriptions %in% most_common_base_description]
  
  # Loop over remaining unique base descriptions
  for (xxx in unique_base_descriptions){
    
    # Find indices where the base description matches the current unique description
    index <- which(base_descriptions %in% xxx) 
    
    # Concatenate the variables associated with this base description into a comma-separated string
    # `index` identifies the relevant variable labels from `variables`
    variable_list <- paste0(variables[index], collapse = ", ") 
    
    # Create a string combining the base description and its variables
    variable_description <- paste0(xxx, ":- ", variable_list) 
    
    # Append this description to the results list
    base_results <- append(base_results, variable_description)
    
  }
  
  }

    # Most common last. 
    base_results <- rev(base_results)
    
  # Return the list of prepared base descriptions
    return(base_results)

}

write_function(prepare_base_for_table, folder = "Scripts")


Example applications of prepare_base_for_table.

prepare_base_for_table(base_test1)

[[1]]
[1] "Asked of all respondents:- Sex of respondent"

[[2]]
[1] "All other variables:-  self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents"

prepare_base_for_table(base_test2)

[[1]]
[1] "Self-completion questionnaire, asked of a random sub-set of respondents"

title: 4  Helper functions
text:
4.4 Support functions for unweighted_median


4.4.1 A function for grouped median SE
Purpose: this function will be used with unweighted_median to calculate an unweighted standard error of a median within groups.
Variables to use for testing…

df2 %>% names

 [1] "RAgeCat"   "RSex"      "skipmeal"  "leftrigh"  "welfare2"  "libauth"  
 [7] "MuchPov"   "WtFactor"  "skipmeal2" "MuchPov2"  "libauth2"  "leftrigh2"
[13] "welfare3" 

df2 %>% count(RSex, RAgeCat) %>% df_to_dense_flextable()

RSexRAgeCatnMale18-2491Male25-34174Male35-44227Male45-54241Male55-59125Male60-64134Male65+460MaleDK/Refused/Not answered2Female18-24111Female25-34255Female35-44298Female45-54302Female55-59124Female60-64127Female65+549FemaleDK/Refused/Not answered4



Arguments:

data - data frame in use by unweighted_median,

outcome - character vector length 1,

predictors - character vector of predictors.


grouped_medianse <- function(data, outcome, predictors){
  
  # Select only the predictor columns from the data
  data_with_required_predictors <- data %>% select(all_of(predictors)) 
  
  # For each predictor column, pull the factor vector from the data and drop unused levels
  predictors_as_list <- map(
    names(data_with_required_predictors), 
    ~data %>% pull(.x) %>% fct_drop
  )
  
  # Split the original data into subsets based on combinations of predictor factor levels
  split_data <- split(data, predictors_as_list, drop = TRUE)
  
  # For each subset of data, calculate the median standard error and create a results row
  results <- map(1:length(split_data), function(number) {
    
    # Extract the outcome variable values for the current subset
    outcome_data <- split_data[[number]] %>% pull(outcome)
    
    # Calculate the median standard error using the wrappedtools package
    median <- wrappedtools::medianse(outcome_data)
    
    # Extract the unique combination of predictor levels for this subset
    levels_in_use <- split_data[[number]] %>% 
      select(all_of(predictors)) %>% 
      unique() %>% 
      # Add a new column with the median standard error estimate
      mutate(estimate_se = median)
    
  }) %>% 
    
    bind_rows() # Combine all results into a single data frame
  
  return(results) # Return the combined data frame with predictor levels and median SE estimates
  
}

write_function(grouped_medianse, folder = "Scripts")


Examples of application, the output is a dataframe with the estimated standard error with the desired naming convention estimate_se which matches that used elsewhere for calculating SE.

4.4.2 A function for grouped median CI
Purpose: this function willb used with unweighted_median to calculate an unweighted confidence interval around a median within groups.
Arguments:

data - data frame in use by unweighted_median,

outcome - character vector length 1,

predictors - character vector of predictors.


grouped_medianci <- function(data, outcome, predictors){
  
  # Select only the predictor columns from the data
  data_with_required_predictors <- data %>% select(all_of(predictors)) 
  
  # For each predictor column, extract its factor values from data and drop unused levels
  predictors_as_list <- map(
    names(data_with_required_predictors), 
    ~data %>% pull(.x) %>% fct_drop
  )
  
  # Split the original data frame into subsets based on the combination of factor levels in predictors_as_list
  split_data <- split(data, predictors_as_list, drop = TRUE)
  
  # For each subset in split_data...
  results <- map(1:length(split_data), function(number) {
    
    # Pull the outcome variable values for this subset
    outcome_data <- split_data[[number]] %>% pull(outcome)
    
    # Proceed only if there are more than 5 observations in the subset
    if(length(outcome_data) > 5){
      
      # Calculate median confidence interval with bootstrap (from wrappedtools package)
      cil <- wrappedtools::median_cl_boot(outcome_data)
      
      # Extract the unique combination of predictor levels for this subset
      levels_in_use <- split_data[[number]] %>% 
        select(all_of(predictors)) %>% 
        unique() %>% 
        # Add the lower and upper confidence interval bounds as new columns
        mutate(estimate_low = cil$CIlow, estimate_upp = cil$CIhigh)
      
      # Return this data frame with predictor levels and confidence intervals
      return(levels_in_use) 
    }
  }) %>% 
    # Combine the list of data frames into one data frame
    bind_rows()
  
  # Return the combined results
  return(results)
  
}

write_function(grouped_medianci, folder = "Scripts")


Examples of use:-
Again, the output is a dataframe with the estimated CI using the naming convention unweighted_median expects, which matches that used elsewhere for calculating CI.

title: 4  Helper functions
text:
4.5 Significance testing
Data filtered on both outcome and predictor variables.
Predictor already a factor. Outcome either already numeric or a factor.
unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat:
* “mean”
* “median”
* “sum”
unweighted_test_cat_by_cat:
* “perc”
* “count”
weighted_test_numeric_by_cat:
* “w_mean”
* “w_median”
* “w_sum”
weighted_test_cat_by_cat:
* “w_perc”
* “w_count”

4.5.1 Numeric variables, unweighted

# df2
# data <- df3
# outcome <- "skipmeal2"
# predictor <- "RSex"

unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x <- data %>% pull(predictor)
  y <- data %>% pull(outcome)
  
  x_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  complete_idx <- complete.cases(y, x)
  y <- y[complete_idx]
  x <- x[complete_idx]
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    # Check normality of residuals (Shapiro-Wilk)
    model <- lm(y ~ x)
    shapiro_p <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value
    
    # Decision
    if (shapiro_p < 0.05) {
      # Non-normal → Kruskal–Wallis
      method_used <- "Kruskal–Wallis"
      p_val <- kruskal.test(y ~ x)$p.value
    } else {
      # Normal enough → Welch's ANOVA
      method_used <- "Welch's ANOVA"
      p_val <- oneway.test(y ~ x, var.equal = FALSE)$p.value
    }
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
    cross_break = predictor,
    predictor1 = predictor, 
    p_lab1 = x_lab, 
    outcome = outcome, 
    o_lab = y_lab,
    p_method = result$method,
    p_value = result$p
  )
  
  result <- map_df(c("mean", "median", "sum"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
}

4.5.2 Numeric variables, weighted

# data <- df_svy
# outcome <- "welfare2"
# predictor <- "RSex"

weighted_test_numeric_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  suppressMessages({
  
  x_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  filtered_data <- data %>% filter(if_all(c(outcome, predictor), ~!is.na(.x)))
  frmla <- paste0(outcome, " ~ ", predictor)
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    # Check normality of residuals (Shapiro-Wilk)
    model <- survey::svyglm(frmla, design = filtered_data)
    shapiro_p <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value
    
    # Decision
    if (shapiro_p < 0.05) {
      # Non-normal → Kruskal–Wallis
      method_used <- "Kruskal–Wallis"
      p_val <- survey::svyranktest(frmla, design = filtered_data)$p.value
    } else {
      # Normal enough → Welch's ANOVA
      method_used <- "Wald Test"
      anova_result <- survey::regTermTest(model, predictor)
      p_val <- anova_result[["p"]] %>% as.vector()
    }
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
      cross_break = predictor,
      predictor1 = predictor, 
      p_lab1 = x_lab, 
      outcome = outcome, 
      o_lab = y_lab,
      p_method = result$method,
      p_value = result$p
    )
  
  result <- map_df(c("w_mean", "w_median", "w_sum"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
  })
}

write_function(weighted_test_numeric_by_cat, folder = "Scripts")

4.5.3 Categorical variables, unweighted

unweighted_test_cat_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x <- data %>% pull(predictor)
  y <- data %>% pull(outcome)
  
  x_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  complete_idx <- complete.cases(y, x)
  y <- y[complete_idx]
  x <- x[complete_idx]
  
  result <- tryCatch({

      # chi-square test
      method_used <- "Chi-Square test"
      p_val <- chisq.test(x, y)$p.value
    
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
      cross_break = predictor,
      predictor1 = predictor, 
      p_lab1 = x_lab, 
      outcome = outcome, 
      o_lab = y_lab,
      p_method = result$method,
      p_value = result$p
     )

  result <- map_df(c("perc", "count"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)

}

4.5.4 Categorical variables, weighted

weighted_test_cat_by_cat <- function(data, outcome, predictor) {
  
  x_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(predictor)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")
  y_lab <- data[["variables"]] %>% select(all_of(outcome)) %>% var_label(unlist = TRUE, null_action = "fill")

  # Remove missing values
  filtered_data <- data %>% filter(if_all(c(outcome, predictor), ~!is.na(.x)))
  frmla <- formula(paste0("~",outcome,  " + " , predictor))
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    
      # chi-square test
      method_used <- "Chi-Square test"
      p_val <- svychisq(frmla, design = filtered_data, statistic = "adjWald")$p.value
      p_val <- p_val %>% as.vector()
      
    list(method = method_used, p = p_val)
    
  }, error = function(e) {
    list(method = "Significance test returned an error.", p = NA)
  })
  
  result <- tibble(
    cross_break = predictor,
    predictor1 = predictor, 
    p_lab1 = x_lab, 
    outcome = outcome, 
    o_lab = y_lab,
    p_method = result$method,
    p_value = result$p
  )

  result <- map_df(c("w_perc", "w_count"), ~result %>% mutate(stat = .x))  
  
  return(result)
  
}

title: 4  Helper functions
text:
4.6 p.values wrapper

return_pvalues <- function(data, outcome, predictor){

    if(any(str_detect(class(data), "survey"))){
      data_2 <- data[["variables"]]
      weights <- "weights"
    } else {
        data_2 <- data
        weights <- "no weights"
    }
  
  outcome_class_factor <- class(data_2 %>% pull(outcome)) %>% str_detect(., "factor") %>% any()
  
  if(weights == "no weights"){
    
    if(outcome_class_factor == TRUE){
      result <- unweighted_test_cat_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    } else { 
      result <- unweighted_test_numeric_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    }
    
  } else {
    
    if(outcome_class_factor == TRUE){
      result <- weighted_test_cat_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor) 
    } else { 
      result <- weighted_test_numeric_by_cat(data = data, outcome = outcome, predictor = predictor)
    }
    
  }
  
  return(result)

}

write_function(return_pvalues, folder = "Scripts")

outcomes <- c("libauth", "RSex", "RAgeCat")
predictors <- c("MuchPov2", "welfare3")

map_return_p_values <- function(outcomes, predictors, data){
  map_df(predictors, function(pred){
    map_df(outcomes, function(out){
      return_pvalues(data = data, outcome = out, predictor = pred)
    })
  })
}

title: 6  3. Calculate statistics
text:
6.2 Statistics functions overview:
Mean, median and sum for numeric outcomes, percentage and count for categorical ones.
Weighted functions:
* weighted_mean,
* weighted_median,
* weighted_sum,
* weighted_perc,
* weighted_count.
Unweighted functions:
* unweighted_mean,
* unweighted_median,
* unweighted_sum,
* unweighted_perc,
* unweighted_count.
An unweighted count is always calculated, stored in unweighted_n. When using unweighted_count it is also stored in estimate (it is duplicated).
Arguments:

data - a dataframe for unweighted functions, a srvyr object for weighted functions. Unweighted functions will also accept srvyr objects.

outcomes - vector of otucome variable names.

predictors - list of predictor variable names. Each element is a vector and can be of length > 1 to produce nested tables.

conf - either “se” for standard errors or “ci” for confidence intervals.

base - the output of the base_information function.

Data output structure:- a data frame, column names are…

cross_break - outcome and predictor variable names separated by ’ X ’

outcome - outcome variable name

o_lab - outcome variable label

o_cat - outcome variable levels, or duplicate of value in stat for numeric statistics.

predictor, p_lab and p_cat - same interpretation as for outcomes, with addition of numbers to differentiate mutliple predictors, predictor1 for the first predictor and so on.

stat - contains the statistic definition.

estimate, contains the statistics.

estimate_se, estimate_stat_low or estimate_stat_upp for standard error, lower and upper confidence intervals.

estimate_ci present when confidence intervals calculated, but not really used at any point.
base - unweighted base size for statistics.

base_description - the output of create_bases for that row’s selection of predictor and outcome variables.

unweighted_n - cell count.

cross_break sometimes missing when only an outcome specified, causes some issues later.
The values of stat can be:

“mean”

“median”

“sum”

“perc”
“count”

“w_mean”

“w_median”

“w_sum”

“w_perc”

“w_count”

title: 6  3. Calculate statistics
text:
6.3 Weighted functions

Numeric variables - weighted_mean.

Numeric variables - weighted_median.

Numeric variables - weighted_sum.

Categorical variables - weighted_perc.

Categorical variables - weighted_count.


6.3.1 Weighted means

# data <- df_svy
# outcomes <- c("libauth","leftrigh")
# predictors <- list("RSex")
# predictors <- list("RSex","RAgeCat")
# cur_outcome <- "libauth"
# cur_predictor <- "RSex"

weighted_mean <- function(
    data, # a srvyr design object
    outcomes, # a character vector of outcome variables
    predictors = NULL, # a list, each element containing a character vector of predictor variables
    conf = NULL, # "se" or "ci"; returns either standard error or confidence intervals
    base = NULL
) {suppressMessages({suppressWarnings({
  
  output <- map(outcomes, function(cur_outcome) {
    
    base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = cur_outcome)) # edit
    
    # Remove missing data
    one_total_table <- data %>% 
      filter(!is.na(.data[[cur_outcome]])) %>% 
      summarise(estimate = survey_mean(!!sym(cur_outcome), vartype = conf), 
                base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
        mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_mean") %>% 
        mutate(outcome = cur_outcome) %>% 
        rename(any_of(c(o_cat = cur_outcome))) %>% 
        mutate(o_lab = attributes(data$variables[[cur_outcome]])$label) %>% 
      # edit
      mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 

    # Create cross-breaks
    if(!is.null(predictors)){
    predictor_tables <- map(predictors, function(cur_predictor) { 
      
            # Handle cases with and without predictor variables
        if (length(cur_predictor) > 0) {
          
          # Vector containing predictors and outcomes
          predictor_and_outcome <- c(cur_predictor, cur_outcome)
          base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = predictor_and_outcome)) # edit
          
          one_predictor_table <- data %>%
            # Filter missing values
            filter(rowSums(across(all_of(predictor_and_outcome), ~ is.na(.))) == 0) %>%
            # Group data
            group_by(across(all_of(cur_predictor))) %>% 
            # Weighted mean + base sizes, with grouping vars
            summarise(estimate = survey_mean(!!sym(cur_outcome), vartype = conf), 
                      base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
            # Added for consistency when merged with categorical outcome tables. 
            mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_mean") %>% 
            # Standardise names so can stack results across different predictors and outcomes. 
            standardise_names(data = data, preds = cur_predictor, out_var = cur_outcome) %>%
            mutate(cross_break = paste0(cur_predictor, collapse = "_X_")) %>%
            # edit
            mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
        
        }
      
      return(one_predictor_table)
      
    })
    
    predictor_tables <- bind_rows(predictor_tables)
    
    } # Predictors ends
    
    # Bind crossbreaks and frequency table together
    if(!is.null(predictors)){table_for_one_outcome <- bind_rows(one_total_table, predictor_tables)
    } else {table_for_one_outcome <- one_total_table}
    
    table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>%
      # Record statistic calculated
      mutate(stat = "w_mean") %>%
      # Add 'Total' to predictor category variables for frequency section of the table.
      mutate(across(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), ~case_when(is.na(.x) ~ "Total", TRUE ~ .x))) %>%
      # Reorder variables
      select(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), outcome, o_lab, o_cat, stat, contains("estimate"), base, base_description) # edit
    
    if(!is.null(conf)){
    if(conf == "ci"){
      table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% 
        mutate(estimate_ci = paste0(as.character(estimate_low), " - ", as.character(estimate_upp)))}
    } else { 
      table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% mutate(estimate_se = "-", .after = estimate)
      }

    return(table_for_one_outcome)
    
  }) # Outcomes loop ends 
  
  output <- output %>% bind_rows() %>% mutate(unweighted_n = base)
  
  return(output)
  
})})
}

6.3.2 Weighted medians

weighted_median <- function(
    data, # a srvyr design object
    outcomes, # a character vector of outcome variables
    predictors = NULL, # a list, each element containing a character vector of predictor variables
    conf = NULL, # "se" or "ci"; returns either standard error or confidence intervals 
    base = NULL
) {suppressMessages({suppressWarnings({
  
  output <- map(outcomes, function(cur_outcome) {
    # Remove missing data
    base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = cur_outcome)) # edit

    one_total_table <- data %>% 
      filter(!is.na(.data[[cur_outcome]])) %>% 
      summarise(estimate = survey_median(!!sym(zap_labels(cur_outcome)), vartype = conf), 
                base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
        mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_median") %>% 
        mutate(outcome = cur_outcome) %>% 
        rename(any_of(c(o_cat = cur_outcome))) %>% 
        mutate(o_lab = attributes(data$variables[[cur_outcome]])$label) %>% 
      mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
  
    # Create cross-breaks
    if(!is.null(predictors)){
    predictor_tables <- map(predictors, function(cur_predictor) { 
      
            # Handle cases with and without predictor variables
        if (length(cur_predictor) > 0) {
          
          # Vector containing predictors and outcomes
          predictor_and_outcome <- c(cur_predictor, cur_outcome)
          base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = predictor_and_outcome))
          
          one_predictor_table <- data %>%
            # Filter missing values
            filter(rowSums(across(all_of(predictor_and_outcome), ~ is.na(.))) == 0) %>%
            # Group data
            group_by(across(all_of(cur_predictor))) %>% 
            # Weighted median + base sizes, with grouping vars
            summarise(estimate = survey_median(!!sym(zap_labels(cur_outcome)), vartype = conf),
                      base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
            # Added for consistency when merged with categorical outcome tables. 
            mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_median") %>% 
            # Standardise names so can stack results across different predictors and outcomes. 
            standardise_names(data = data, preds = cur_predictor, out_var = cur_outcome) %>%
            mutate(cross_break = paste0(cur_predictor, collapse = "_X_")) %>%
            mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
        }
      
      return(one_predictor_table)
      
    })
    
    predictor_tables <- bind_rows(predictor_tables)
    
    } # Predictors ends
    
    # Bind crossbreaks and frequency table together
    if(!is.null(predictors)){table_for_one_outcome <- bind_rows(one_total_table, predictor_tables)
    } else {table_for_one_outcome <- one_total_table}
    
    table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>%
      # Record statistic calculated
      mutate(stat = "w_median") %>%
      # Add 'Total' to predictor category variables for frequency section of the table.
      mutate(across(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), ~case_when(is.na(.x) ~ "Total", TRUE ~ .x))) %>%
      # Reorder variables
      select(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), outcome, o_lab, o_cat, stat, contains("estimate"), base, base_description) 
    
    if(!is.null(conf)){
      if(conf == "ci"){
        table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% 
          mutate(estimate_ci = paste0(as.character(estimate_low), " - ", as.character(estimate_upp)))}
    } else { 
      table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% mutate(estimate_se = "-", .after = estimate)
    }

    return(table_for_one_outcome)
    
  }) # Outcomes loop ends 
  
  output <- output %>% bind_rows() %>% mutate(unweighted_n = base)
  
  return(output)
  
})})
}

write_function(weighted_median, folder = ddir)

6.3.3 Weighted sums

weighted_sum <- function(
    data, # a srvyr design object
    outcomes, # a character vector of outcome variables
    predictors = NULL, # a list, each element containing a character vector of predictor variables
    conf = NULL, # "se" or "ci"; returns either standard error or confidence intervals 
    base = NULL
) {suppressMessages({suppressWarnings({
  
  output <- map(outcomes, function(cur_outcome) {
    
    base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = cur_outcome)) 
    
    # Remove missing data
    one_total_table <- data %>% 
      filter(!is.na(.data[[cur_outcome]])) %>% 
      summarise(estimate = survey_total(!!sym(zap_labels(cur_outcome)), vartype = conf), 
                base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
        mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_sum") %>%
        mutate(outcome = cur_outcome) %>% 
        rename(any_of(c(o_cat = cur_outcome))) %>% 
        mutate(o_lab = attributes(data$variables[[cur_outcome]])$label) %>% 
      mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
  
    # Create cross-breaks
    if(!is.null(predictors)){
    predictor_tables <- map(predictors, function(cur_predictor) { 
      
            # Handle cases with and without predictor variables
        if (length(cur_predictor) > 0) {
          
          # Vector containing predictors and outcomes
          predictor_and_outcome <- c(cur_predictor, cur_outcome)
          base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = predictor_and_outcome)) 
          
          one_predictor_table <- data %>%
            # Filter missing values
            filter(rowSums(across(all_of(predictor_and_outcome), ~ is.na(.))) == 0) %>%
            # Group data
            group_by(across(all_of(cur_predictor))) %>% 
            # Weighted median + base sizes, with grouping vars
            summarise(estimate = survey_total(!!sym(zap_labels(cur_outcome)), vartype = conf),
                      base = unweighted(n())) %>% 
            # Added for consistency when merged with categorical outcome tables. 
            mutate("{cur_outcome}" := "w_sum") %>%
            # Standardise names so can stack results across different predictors and outcomes. 
            standardise_names(data = data, preds = cur_predictor, out_var = cur_outcome) %>%
            mutate(cross_break = paste0(cur_predictor, collapse = "_X_")) %>%
            mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
        }
      
      return(one_predictor_table)
      
    })
    
    predictor_tables <- bind_rows(predictor_tables)
    
    } # Predictors ends
    
    # Bind crossbreaks and frequency table together
    if(!is.null(predictors)){table_for_one_outcome <- bind_rows(one_total_table, predictor_tables)
    } else {table_for_one_outcome <- one_total_table}
    
    table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>%
      mutate(stat = "w_sum") %>%
      # Add 'Total' to predictor category variables for frequency section of the table.
      mutate(across(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), ~case_when(is.na(.x) ~ "Total", TRUE ~ .x))) %>%
      # Reorder variables
      select(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), outcome, o_lab, o_cat, stat, contains("estimate"), base, base_description) 
        
    if(!is.null(conf)){
      if(conf == "ci"){
        table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% 
          mutate(estimate_ci = paste0(as.character(estimate_low), " - ", as.character(estimate_upp)))}
    } else { 
      table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% mutate(estimate_se = "-", .after = estimate)
    }

    return(table_for_one_outcome)
    
  }) # Outcomes loop ends 
  
  output <- output %>% bind_rows() %>% mutate(unweighted_n = base)
  
  return(output)
  
})})
}

write_function(weighted_sum, folder = ddir)

6.3.4 Weighted percentages

weighted_perc <- function(
    data, # a srvyr design object
    outcomes, # a character vector of outcome variables
    predictors = NULL, # a list, each element containing a character vector of predictor variables
    conf = NULL, # "se" or "ci"; returns either standard error or confidence intervals 
    base = NULL
) {suppressMessages({suppressWarnings({
  
  output <- map(outcomes, function(cur_outcome) {
    
    base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = cur_outcome)) # edit
    
    # Remove missing data
    one_total_table <- data %>% 
      filter(!is.na(.data[[cur_outcome]])) %>% 
      group_by(across(all_of(cur_outcome))) %>% 
      summarise(estimate = survey_prop(vartype = conf), 
                unweighted_n = unweighted(n())) %>% 
      mutate(base = sum(unweighted_n, na.rm = T)) %>%
      mutate(outcome = cur_outcome) %>% 
      rename(any_of(c(o_cat = cur_outcome))) %>% 
      mutate(o_lab = attributes(data$variables[[cur_outcome]])$label) %>% 
      mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
  
    # Create cross-breaks
    if(!is.null(predictors)){
    predictor_tables <- map(predictors, function(cur_predictor) { 
  
            # Handle cases with and without predictor variables
        if (length(cur_predictor) > 0) {
          
          # Vector containing predictors and outcomes
          predictor_and_outcome <- c(cur_predictor, cur_outcome)
          base_defintion <- ifelse(is.null(base), NA, create_bases(base_info = base, variables = predictor_and_outcome)) # edit
          
          one_predictor_table <- data %>%
            # Filter missing values
            filter(rowSums(across(all_of(predictor_and_outcome), ~ is.na(.))) == 0) %>%
            # Group data
            group_by(across(all_of(predictor_and_outcome))) %>% 
            # Weighted percentage + base sizes, with grouping vars
            summarise(estimate = survey_prop(vartype = conf), 
                      unweighted_n = unweighted(n())) %>% 
            mutate(base = sum(unweighted_n, na.rm = T)) %>%
            # Standardise names so can stack results across different predictors and outcomes. 
            standardise_names(data = data, preds = cur_predictor, out_var = cur_outcome) %>%
            mutate(cross_break = paste0(cur_predictor, collapse = "_X_")) %>%
            mutate(base_description = base_defintion) # descriptor label for base 
        }
      
      return(one_predictor_table)
      
    })
    
    predictor_tables <- bind_rows(predictor_tables)
    
    } # Predictors ends
    
    # Bind crossbreaks and frequency table together
    if(!is.null(predictors)){table_for_one_outcome <- bind_rows(one_total_table, predictor_tables)
    } else {table_for_one_outcome <- one_total_table}
    
    table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>%
      mutate(stat = "w_perc") %>%
      # Add 'Total' to predictor category variables for frequency section of the table.
      mutate(across(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), ~case_when(is.na(.x) ~ "Total", TRUE ~ .x))) %>%
      # Reorder variables
      select(contains(c("cross_break", "predictor", "p_lab", "p_cat")), outcome, o_lab, o_cat, stat, contains("estimate"), base, base_description, unweighted_n) 
    
    if(!is.null(conf)){
      if(conf == "ci"){
        table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% 
          mutate(estimate_ci = paste0(as.character(estimate_low), " - ", as.character(estimate_upp)))}
    } else { 
      table_for_one_outcome <- table_for_one_outcome %>% mutate(estimate_se = "-", .after = estimate)
    }

    return(table_for_one_outcome)
    
  }) # Outcomes loop ends 
  
  output <- output %>% bind_rows() %>% mutate(unweighted_n = base)
  
  return(output)
  
})})
}

write_function(weighted_perc, folder = ddir)
